greeting: Morning, Haniel. Monday — clean slate, full tank, let's go.
hype: GS is 88% done and Module is one hardening sprint from its mockup gate. Today you move both needles. Let's run it.
notice: Snapshots are 2 days old (synced 2026-06-27) — quick scan before diving in.

tasks.title: Bonus formula definition (production + paint shop)
tasks.notes: KB 9.1 + 12.13. Private session with Guilherme/Mayara. Production: target days + quality gate. Paint: team-based + labor split rule (equal vs weighted).

prep.what: Define and document the bonus formula for production and paint shop teams — targets, eligibility gates, and payout rules.
prep.understand: The production bonus requires: (a) on-time delivery vs. `target_days` per project type, and (b) CK2 quality gate pass. The paint shop bonus is team-based with a labor-split rule (equal division vs. weighted by labor hours). t38 (the bonus system code) is blocked until this formula is locked. The numbers come from Guilherme/Mayara — your job today is to draft the structure so the session fills in the blanks, not start from zero.
approach: Pull up KB 9.1 and KB 12.13 — note what target days and quality gate specs are already documented vs. still blank.

Draft a production bonus template: `eligible = (actual_days <= target_days) AND ck2_pass; payout = eligible ? formula_amount : 0` — leave `target_days` and `formula_amount` as TBD placeholders per project type.
Draft a paint shop template: `team_pool / (equal OR weighted_by_labor_hours)` — mark the split rule as a Guilherme decision.
Schedule a 30-min session with Guilherme + Mayara, share the draft doc, fill in the real numbers, then commit the final spec to the KB.

tasks.title: Design + installation time benchmarks per project type
tasks.notes: KB 9.1: Track next 10 projects for design time. Interview install crew leads for installation times. Required for capacity planning + accurate scheduling.

prep.what: Establish per-project-type time benchmarks for design and installation phases — the empirical data that drives capacity planning in `ProductionPriorityService`.
prep.understand: Two data sources needed: (1) historical ERP/ClickUp project data for design phase durations; (2) install crew lead interviews for field install times. Start with the historical pull — you can do that solo today. Crew interviews need to be scheduled separately. These benchmarks feed `ProductionPriorityService` capacity gating and estimate accuracy.
approach: Export completed projects from ERP — filter by project type, extract design phase start/end dates, compute average duration per type.
Build a benchmark table: `project_type | design_days_p50 | install_days_p50 | sample_count` — start with what the data shows.
Draft 5–7 interview questions for install crew leads ('How long does a standard kitchen install take? What is the biggest time variable?').
Schedule 15-min calls with at least 2 crew leads this week — block the time now while you have momentum.

tasks.title: CK1 / CK2 / CK3 / CK4 inspection item lists
tasks.notes: KB 12.3. CK1 already seeded with placeholder; expand. CK2 (fabrication) + CK3 (cosmetic) need Eric. CK4 (install) needs install crew lead.

prep.what: Write the full pass/fail checklist items for each of the 4 QC stages: CK1 (receipt/pre-fab), CK2 (fabrication), CK3 (cosmetic/finish), CK4 (installation).
prep.understand: CK2 pass is the production bonus eligibility gate — so items must be crisp, binary pass/fail criteria. CK1 is already seeded in the ERP as placeholders. CK2 and CK3 require Eric (fabrication). CK4 requires an install crew lead. Your job today: draft everything from domain knowledge, then route each section to the right reviewer.
approach: Open the ERP `ChecklistTemplate` seeder — read existing CK1 placeholder items to understand the format.
Draft CK2 (fabrication): dimensions match spec, correct species/thickness, joinery tight, surfaces sanded to correct grit, no tearout or defects.
Draft CK3 (cosmetic): finish uniformity, color matches approved sample, no runs/drips/fish-eyes, hardware holes aligned.
Draft CK4 (installation): cabinets level and plumb, doors/drawers operate freely, all hardware torqued, client walkthrough sign-off.
Email draft CK2/CK3 to Eric and CK4 to an install crew lead — ask for corrections and any missing critical items.

tasks.title: Build persona-driven demo scripts for Monday (Rony) + Eduardo follow-up
tasks.phase: Phase 71 · Client demo 2026-06-12 (Eduardo + Mayara + team) — feedback
tasks.notes: Owner's plan: segmented per-sector sessions. Monday = Rony (process/status — owns ClickUp today). Then Eduardo follow-up (financial visibility). Persona-driven.

prep.what: Write two tight demo scripts — one for Rony (daily ops/status) and one for Eduardo (financial visibility) — each under 15 minutes with no feature tours.
prep.understand: Rony owns ClickUp today. His pain is status visibility and job assignment. Show him his day in the ERP. Eduardo's pain is financial visibility — where is the money, per job. Don't show features, show their day. Each script needs a clear 'what changes for you' close. Rony's session is Monday so this needs to be ready today.
approach: Rony script: 'Day in Rony's life' — morning queue check → pick up a job → update status → check the weekly schedule. Max 3 screens.
Eduardo script: 'End-of-week check' — dashboard revenue + margin → drill into one job P&L → share or export a report. Max 3 screens.
End each script with 'What you stop doing in ClickUp' — one bullet per current ClickUp habit being replaced.
Stage the demo with realistic GS project names, dollar amounts, and statuses — it must feel like their actual company, not a demo sandbox.
resources: Eduardo's financial questions from previous demo sessions

tasks.title: Adopt template Clients module into the registry (toggleable)
tasks.notes: Wire the existing template Clients module into the registry so it can be toggled. First real module proving the spine contract.

prep.what: Register the existing Clients module in `ModuleRegistry` so the spine (middleware, nav, permissions) can gate it — proving the contract works on a real module.
prep.understand: The registry contract (t1) and the gating machinery (t3, t4, t5, t6) are all done. The Clients module already has routes/controllers/views from the template. The gap is one registration call. After this, t13 (Clients UX-max) and t14 (spine proof test) both become eligible. This is the acceptance gate for Phase 1.
approach: Open the module registry config file — add a Clients entry: `key: 'clients', label: 'Clients', icon: 'Users', permissions: ['clients.view', 'clients.manage'], nav: '/clients', enabled: true`.
Wrap the Clients routes in a module-gated route group: `Route::module('clients', function() { ... })`.
Toggle the module off in the admin panel — confirm the nav link disappears and all Clients routes return 404.
Run `php artisan test` — the full suite must stay green.

tasks.title: Per-module settings UI (labels, statuses, options)
tasks.notes: Config form per module reading the settings layer — rename labels, define statuses/pipeline stages, edit seedable option lists. Zero-hardcode in practice.

prep.what: Build the per-module settings config form inside the Module Manager — super-admins can rename labels, define pipeline statuses, and edit option lists, all persisted through `SettingsManager`.
prep.understand: `SettingsManager` (t2) is a scoped key/value store with per-module namespacing. The Module Manager UI (t8) is done. This task adds a 'Configure' tab per module. After it ships, t11 (UX-max pass on the Module Manager) becomes unblocked. This is what lets a new client rebrand 'Clients' to 'Customers' from the UI without a code deploy.
approach: Add a settings route under the Module Manager: `/admin/modules/{key}/settings` → `ModuleSettingsController@show/update`.
Build three form sections: Labels (rename module label + singular record label), Statuses (add/reorder/rename pipeline stages), Options (editable seeded lists).
Wire form submit to `SettingsManager::setMany(['clients.label' => ..., 'clients.stages' => [...]])` and return a flash success.
Seed sample config keys for the Clients module — test that changing the label propagates to nav text and breadcrumbs live.
resources: Branding settings (t10) as reference pattern for form shape

tasks.title: Wire real audit logging for access + config changes
tasks.notes: BLOCKER: syncRoles/syncPermissions write the pivot directly with no model events. Setting/ModuleState writes emit zero audit data. Needs explicit activity() logging at every write site.

prep.what: Add explicit audit log entries at every role/permission write, module state toggle, and settings change — creating the 'who changed what' trail the mockup phase timeline assumes exists.
prep.understand: Laravel's `LogsActivity` trait fires on Eloquent model events. But `syncRoles()`/`syncPermissions()` bypass events entirely (direct pivot writes). `SettingsManager::set()` and `ModuleState::setEnabled()` also emit nothing. You cannot fix this with a trait — you need manual `activity()->causedBy()->log()` calls inserted at each write site.
approach: Find all `syncRoles()`/`syncPermissions()` call sites (`UserController`, `RoleController`) — capture old roles → sync → record before/after in a single `activity('rbac')` entry.
Find `SettingsManager::set()` and `setMany()` — add `activity('settings')->causedBy(auth()->user())->withProperties(['key' => ..., 'old' => ..., 'new' => ...])->log('setting changed')`.
Find `ModuleState::setEnabled()` — add audit entry: actor, module key, old enabled state, new enabled state.
Write `test_role_change_creates_audit_record` in Pest: call `syncRoles()` on a user, assert one activity log entry was created.
